# Why nutrition matters

> *Why should a medical student take nutrition seriously? The answer runs through the six
> pillars of health, with nutrition as the hub that connects them.*

## The shape of modern disease

For most of medicine's history, the doctor's enemy was the acute and the infectious:
pneumonia, sepsis, trauma. That enemy has not gone away, but it has been overtaken. Today,
**non-communicable diseases (NCDs)** cause around **three-quarters of all deaths worldwide**
[@who2020diet]: cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, cancers, chronic respiratory disease.
These are, in large part, **slow diseases of daily living**, driven by a handful of *modifiable*
behaviours: what we eat, how much we move, whether we sleep, smoke or drink.

Diet sits near the top of that list. The Global Burden of Disease study attributes around
**11 million deaths a year** to dietary risks such as too much sodium, too few whole grains and
too little fruit, making diet one of the single largest *modifiable* contributors to early death
[@gbd2019diet].

::: {.callout-note}
## Read the headline carefully
The "11 million deaths" figure is a modelled estimate from observational data, and how heavily it
weights sodium is debated. Read it as a signal that diet is among the leading modifiable risks; it
is not a precise body count.
:::

This reframes the job. A clinician who can only treat the acute crisis arrives, by definition,
late. The larger opportunity, and the harder skill, is to **prevent and slow** chronic disease.
Nutrition is where that work begins.

## The six pillars of health

Protecting health means more than a vague instruction to "live better." It rests on a small set of
evidence-based behaviours that prevent, treat and sometimes reverse chronic disease
[@aclm2022comp; @lianov2010], used **alongside** drugs and surgery, never instead of them. There
are **six pillars**:

1. **Nutrition** — a predominantly whole-food, plant-rich eating pattern.
2. **Physical activity** — regular movement across the week.
3. **Restorative sleep** — adequate quantity and quality.
4. **Stress management** — recognising and regulating chronic stress.
5. **Positive social connection** — relationships and community.
6. **Avoidance of risky substances** — tobacco, excess alcohol.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Prevention, the Singapore way
In Singapore, this preventive, behaviour-first approach is increasingly the organising frame of
primary care, most visibly through **Healthier SG**, the national shift from reactive hospital care
toward proactive, relationship-based prevention [@mohHealthierSG]. The conviction behind **Health in
Medicine** is that helping people live well is core clinical work.
:::

## Nutrition as the hub

Of the six pillars, nutrition holds a special place: it **connects to all the others**, in both
directions. It is the natural spine of this course, because pulling on it tends to move the whole
web.

<div data-island="pillars-hub" data-src="/diagrams/pillars-hub.yml"></div>

- **Nutrition ↔ physical activity.** Diet and movement act together on weight and glucose; neither
  alone is as effective as both.
- **Nutrition ↔ sleep.** Short sleep lowers insulin sensitivity (how well the body clears glucose
  in response to insulin) and raises appetite and intake of energy-dense food, while late, heavy
  meals worsen sleep. The result is a self-reinforcing loop [@sleepRestriction].
- **Nutrition ↔ stress.** Acute and chronic stress, acting through cortisol (the main stress
  hormone), are thought to drive "comfort eating" of palatable, energy-dense food [@dallman2003].
- **Nutrition ↔ risky substances.** Alcohol is itself dietary: empty energy that also disinhibits
  food choice.
- **Nutrition ↔ social connection.** Eating is profoundly social, through family meals, communal
  hawker eating and festive food. Social context can entrench or transform a diet.

One practical rule follows: **start with nutrition, but plan across the pillars.** Fixing the diet
often means touching sleep and stress too.

## Patterns over nutrients

A persistent trap for patients and doctors alike is **single-nutrient reductionism**: the search
for the one villain (carbs, fat, gluten) or the one "superfood." The evidence points elsewhere.
What predicts health is the **overall dietary pattern** rather than isolated nutrients [@aha2021diet].
Two of the most-studied patterns illustrate the point:

- The **Mediterranean** pattern (olive oil, nuts, legumes, fish, vegetables) was associated with
  about a **30% lower risk** on a combined measure of heart-disease events in the PREDIMED trial
  [@estruch2018predimed].
- The **DASH** pattern (rich in fruit, vegetables and low-fat dairy) lowered blood pressure
  meaningfully, even without weight loss or salt restriction [@appel1997dash].

::: {.callout-warning}
## Landmark trials come with caveats
Both results come with limits you will examine in the clinical chapters. PREDIMED's headline comes
from a **2018 re-analysis** after the original had problems with how patients were assigned to
groups, and its main result combined several heart-disease events, most of them strokes. DASH was a
short controlled-feeding study that measured blood pressure, a marker, rather than heart attacks
themselves. Both are real findings, correctly bounded, which is how any landmark trial should be
read.
:::

## Why this is Singapore's fight

Diet-related disease is a declared national priority. The **War on Diabetes** (launched 2016) treats
diabetes as a population-scale problem [@mohWarOnDiabetes]: roughly **1 in 3** Singaporeans has a
lifetime risk of diabetes, with higher risk among Malay and Indian residents.

The **National Nutrition Survey 2022** shows why the thresholds matter. The population diet sits
some distance from the guidelines [@mohNNS2022]:

| Finding (NNS 2022) | What it means |
|---|---|
| Only **~4%** of staples eaten are wholegrain | A *quality* gap (refined rice and noodles dominate) |
| **~9 in 10** residents exceed the WHO sodium limit | Most sodium hides in sauces, broths and food eaten out |

Singapore translates guidelines into tools you will use as counselling anchors all year:

- **My Healthy Plate** — the "**quarter, quarter, half**": ¼ wholegrains, ¼ protein, ½ fruit and
  vegetables [@hpbHealthyPlate].
- **Nutri-Grade** — the A–D front-of-pack grade on drinks, by sugar and saturated fat; the labels
  on *teh tarik* and bubble tea you already recognise [@hpbNutriGrade].

These let advice stay concrete and local: a *cai png* (economy rice) plate becomes "two veg, one
protein, less gravy"; a Grade-D drink becomes *kopi-o kosong* or plain water.

## A learnable, examinable clinical skill

If nutrition matters this much, why is it so often neglected in the clinic? Largely because doctors
are **under-trained** in it: graduates across many countries report feeling under-prepared to give
dietary advice [@crowley2019]. That gap is why this course exists. Nutrition counselling is a
**core, examinable, learnable** competency, and a behaviour you can assess and help change
[@frost2018mi]. A patient's diet is a set of habits, shaped by environment and circumstance, that
responds to skilled advice.

::: {.callout-important}
## What this year will, and won't, do
**Will:** build your nutritional literacy — the science, the Singapore context, and the skill of
advising real patients well. **Won't (yet):** turn you into a dietitian or hand you prescribing
protocols. Detailed medical nutrition therapy, drug choices and disease management come later, along
with when to refer. At this stage, nutrition is about **motivation, concepts and the clinical
conversation**, taught with humility about what the evidence does and does not show.
:::

## Why a doctor should care about food

Nutrition is where the largest and most modifiable share of modern disease is decided, and unlike
much of medicine, it is something a patient can actually change. Diet is among the leading modifiable
risks for the diseases that now cause most death. It pulls on every other pillar of health, so
improving it tends to move the whole web. And advising on it well is a learnable, examinable clinical
skill. Treating nutrition as core clinical work is the starting point for everything that follows in
this book.

## Check your understanding

A quick self-test on the framing above. Your answers are saved locally in your browser.

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/why-nutrition-matters.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: mapping the pillars

Concepts become useful at the bedside. Meet **Mr Tan**, who feels "basically fine." Practise seeing
the whole picture, not only the plate. Your choices branch the case.

::: {.callout-tip}
Progress is saved locally — replay to explore the other paths.
:::

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/pillar-mapping-mr-tan.case.yml"></div>
